quoteFirst: For the second time I volunteered on the forlorn-hope. After having received a double allowance of grog, we fell in
quoteTime: about eight o’clock in the evening
quoteLast: , 6th April, 1812. The stormers were composed of men from the different regiments of the light division.
title:
Adventures of a Soldier, Written by Himself
Being the Memoirs of Edward Costello, K.S.F. Formerly a Non-Commissioned Officer in the Rifle Brigade, Late Captain in the British Legion, and Now One of the Wardens of the Tower of London; Comprising Narratives of the Campaigns in the Peninsula under the Duke of Wellington, and the Subsequent Civil Wars in Spain.
author: Costello, Edward, 1788-1869

quoteFirst: We left Niagara early on Saturday morning, stopped in Buffalo just long enough August 19, 1848 to go from one rail-way station to another, and reached Syracuse at
quoteLast: , a distance of two hundred miles. That day's journey seems more like a dream than reality, for hills and valleys, woods and meadows, hamlets and villages, lakes and rivers, the puff of the engine, the rattle of the train, men, women, and children in serried ranks, are all mingled in confusion in the kaleidescope of memory, and nothing but a map or a Traveler's Guide-book can unravel the tangled skein of localities that was spun out in that rapid journey of fourteen hours.
title:
The Pictorial Field-Book of the Revolution, Vol. 1 (of 2)
or, Illustrations, by Pen And Pencil, of the History, Biography, Scenery, Relics, and Traditions of the War for Independence
author: Lossing, Benson John, 1813-1891

quoteFirst: At a distance it seemed a mere pond or lake, and a drove of cattle passing over it looked as if they were walking in the water. We rolled on rapidly all day, passed through Balgarha, Kodurseve, and Pakra, timing every post and noting every village with a particularity which it would be tedious here to repeat, and at
quoteLast: dashed into the little town of Vosnezeuski, one hundred and thirty versts from Odessa. Here we came to a dead stand.
title: Incidents of Travel in Greece, Turkey, Russia, and Poland, Vol. 2 (of 2)
author: Stephens, John L., 1805-1852

quoteFirst: It was a little farm-house, surrounded with about twenty acres of vineyard, about as much corn, and close to the house, on one side, was a _potagerie_ of an acre and a half, full of everything which could make plenty in a French peasant's house; and, on the other side, was a little wood, which furnished wherewithal to dress it. It was
quoteLast: when I got to the house, so I left the postillion to manage his point as he could; and, for mine, I walked directly into the house.
title:
The Bed-Book of Happiness
Being a colligation or assemblage of cheerful writings brought together from many quarters into this one compass for the diversion, distraction, and delight of those who lie abed,—a friend to the invalid, a companion to the sleepless, an excuse to the tired
author: Begbie, Harold, 1871-1929

quoteLast: , a light breeze springing up, which fixed at S.S.E., we steered N.W. and N.N.W., round Cape Newenham, which, at noon next day, bore S. by E., distant four leagues.
title: A General History and Collection of Voyages and Travels, Volume 16
author: Kerr, Robert, 1755-1813

quoteFirst: =12th January.=—Lat. 46° 55′ N., long. 10° 41′ W. Wind S.E. Distance 269 miles.
quoteLast: an alarm of fire was given and great excitement prevailed throughout the ship. This danger was caused by a drunken woman in the second cabin, who set fire to her bonnet; it was soon extinguished and the woman put in irons and confined in the “black hole” for the night as a warning.
title: The Colonial Clippers
author: Lubbock, Basil, 1876-1944

quoteFirst: —Hadji Salah to-day saw the sultan, and I was informed by the master of my house that he was to return to Kano in four days after this. In the evening, Hadji Salah, having asked the Gadado’s permission to pay me a visit, came
quoteLast: : he was not watched, that I could see. He informed me that he had seen the sultan to-day, who had desired him to tell the truth, whether I had given him the sheik’s present, or any thing for the sheik before I left Kano.
title: Journal of a second expedition into the interior of Africa, from the Bight of Benin to Soccatoo
author: Clapperton, Hugh, 1788-1827; Salamé, Abraham V. [Contributor]; Barrow, John, Sir, 1764-1848 [Contributor]; Clapperton, Samuel [Contributor]; Lander, Richard, 1804-1834 [Contributor]

quoteFirst: On the 12th of December, after marching five miles through a heavy fall of snow to the village of Tashgang, crossing the river by a wooden bridge close to the village, a violent storm of wind and snow-drift, blowing directly down the valley, compelled me to halt for the night. The snow-storm continued till
quoteLast: , when the weather cleared, and the night was clear and starlight. Next morning, the weather continuing fine, I was able to proceed to Dras.
title:
Western Himalaya and Tibet
A Narrative of a Journey Through the Mountains of Northern India During the Years 1847-8
author: Thomson, Thomas, 1817-1878

quoteTime: About eight o'clock in the evening
quoteLast: his wife went to bed; but William made up a warm fire in the stone fireplace, fed the cattle, and lay down before it. At twelve o'clock he went out, fed the cattle again, and called his wife, who got his breakfast, and he set out.
title: The Turning of the Tide; Or, Radcliffe Rich and His Patients
author: Kellogg, Elijah, 1813-1901

quoteLast: Lamp burns on table._ MRS. WARNER _comes up-stairs, puts her head inside the room nervously, then enters and looks about._
title: The Flutter of the Goldleaf, and Other Plays
author: Dargan, Olive Tilford, 1869-1968; Peterson, Frederick, 1859-1938
